attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with an eight-month history of increasing fatigue. She is an ex-competitive swimmer. She struggles to engage in activities of daily living and does not have the energy to engage in much physical activity. When she has tried to exercise, she has felt excessively tired afterwards and had to call in sick at work for the next two days. In addition to difficulty attending work, she reports poor concentration. She does not feel refreshed when she wakes in the morning. She experiences postural dizziness and feels she has gained weight recently. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears tired. Her pulse rate is 67/min when standing, and 69/min when sitting. Her blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg lying down and 112/72 mmHg standing. Her respiratory rate is 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her BMI is 27 kg/m2. Her chest and abdominal examinations are unremarkable. She has no joint or muscle tenderness on palpation. She has no rashes.

Previous investigations show very mildly elevated ESR and CRP. Other routine blood tests including a full blood count, urea and electrolytes, liver function tests, thyroid function tests, iron studies, HbA1c, vitamin D, and lipid profile are all within normal limits. Epstein-Barr virus serology (VCA IgM and IgG and EBNA) are negative.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is most consistent with myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS). The key features supporting this diagnosis include: substantial reduction in ability to engage in pre-illness level of activity persisting for more than six months, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive difficulties (poor concentration), and orthostatic intolerance (dizziness on standing). The extensive investigations performed have ruled out other common causes of fatigue, which is an important step in diagnosing ME/CFS.

Obstructive sleep apnoea could explain unrefreshing sleep and potentially be related to weight gain. However, it would not account for post-exertional malaise or orthostatic symptoms. Moreover, there's no mention of typical OSA symptoms such as snoring or witnessed apnoeas.

Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) can cause fatigue and orthostatic symptoms. However, a key diagnostic criterion is an increase in heart rate of at least 30 beats per minute when moving from a recumbent to standing position, which is not observed in this patient.

Infectious mononucleosis is unlikely given the duration of symptoms and negative Epstein-Barr virus serology. Acute EBV infection is ruled out by negative VCA IgM, and the negative VCA IgG and EBNA suggest no previous EBV infection.

Fibromyalgia shares some symptoms with ME/CFS, including fatigue and cognitive difficulties. However, the hallmark of fibromyalgia is chronic widespread pain and tenderness to palpation, which are not present in this case.

ME/CFS is a diagnosis of exclusion, and it's important to rule out other conditions that can cause similar symptoms. The patient's history, physical examination, and laboratory results are consistent with ME/CFS and have effectively ruled out many other potential causes of her symptoms.
learningPoints: Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) is characterised by substantial reduction in activity, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive difficulties, and orthostatic intolerance.
A thorough investigation to exclude other causes of fatigue is essential before diagnosing ME/CFS.
ME/CFS diagnosis requires symptoms to persist for at least six months and not be explained by another condition.
references: Myalgic encephalomyelitis (or encephalopathy)/chronic fatigue syndrome: diagnosis and management. In: NICE. 2021. Available at: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng206

Gluckman SJ. Treatment of myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome. In: UpToDate [online]. Accessed 2022 Jun 23. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/treatment-of-myalgic-encephalomyelitis-chronic-fatigue-syndrome
Epstein-Barr virus serology. In: RCPA [online]. 2019; accessed 2022 Aug 16. Available at: https://www.rcpa.edu.au/Manuals/RCPA-Manual/Pathology-Tests/E/Epstein-Barr-virus-serology
